comments: A tape machine malfunction results in the first track being aborted and then replayed after the AC DC track.
Tommy mentions that Rock On the next day will feature a half hour special on Jim Morrison, a decade after his death.

sessions.details: recorded 1981-04-22 for Friday Rock Show. First TX 15 May 1981
recorded 1974-12-10 for John Peel. First TX 1974-12-17

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. Good evening and welcome. This is TV on the radio here. This is the Friday Rock Show.
Right. After a strenuous delve, we've come up with some really good archive material from Gentle Giant, recorded in 1974.
Plus, the repeat of our, in my humble estimation, excellent recent session from Rose Tattoo.
On top of that, we've got some classic cuts from ACDC, Led Zeppelin, Black Sabbath and Yes, among others,
and some new stuff from Johnny Warman, Heritage, Blackfoot, Saxon and Blue Oyster Cult.
And, of course, our little Friday night teaser called The Friday Night Connection.
But this evening, we start with this.
A medley of Australian folk music.
Sorry about the sort of end of Rose Tattoo, because we had a little machine breakdown,
but then we fixed it and we got the end tacked on to the end of ACDC, if you know what I mean.
never want to shortchange anybody
it's 21 minutes after 10
you heard Let There Be Rock by ACDC
from the album called If You Want Blood You've Got It
and that was specially for a whole stack of people
who know a lady by the name of Vicky Knox
who lives in Bolton in Lancashire
and particularly John Grierson who's up there in Durham
someplace
also for David Barnes who lives in Anleby Common
in Hull
ACDC was dedicated to Mike Breakin
of Kemne Aberdeenshire in Scotland
and also Steve Helmut Weaver
and Alan Dennett
and Curly from Crawley in, or rather,
Ifield in Crawley, or Crawley in Ifield.
And I must say, thank you very much indeed
to this lady by the name of Miss K.E. Samuelson
and everybody else who's attending the latter stages
of a blue and white beach party down in Hastings.
Great bit of typing. Hope you enjoyed the music.
ACDC did nice boys, don't like rock and roll.
That was me getting confused. It was Rose Tattoo who did that.
Right, that was Les Zeppelin from the album Presence.
It was called Hearts On For Nowhere.
It's 10.32, the Friday Rock Show from Radio 1.
Played that track especially for a gentleman who's having a holiday at the moment.
His name is Colin Brazier.
Lots of love to Colin Brazier from Mrs. J. Brazier,
who is in Harrow Hill, Romford, Essex.
Loves him very much and says,
she'll always be by your side.
Also played that Zeppelin track with Dave Howarth.
And all the rock miners, it says here,
at Camborne School of Mines.
Everybody in Dip One and the football team.
Andrew Boyd requested a bit of music by Led Zeppelin.
So indeed did gnome on behalf of her boyfriend Teague,
who's got a birthday today, 16.
He's just done his O-levels.
Hope you passed him.
Happy birthday, Teague.
Also Richard Horton of number 40, Woodthorne Road,
Tetton Hall, Wolverhampton,
said say hello to Rick and Humpo and Ken and Banny and Sabode
and Jack and Arch and Tash and Gaz,
Robbo, Chris, Sag, Ian, Paz, Waiter, Nickel, Stell,
the Piraces, I think that is,
Esther, John, Jill or Gil,
Russ, Brother Matthew and everybody else
who knows that man who writes long letters.
And the track before Zeppelin was by Johnny Warman.
It's from a new album out on Rocket.
The album's called Walking Into Mirrors,
A Dangerous Practice.
It was entitled Fantastic Light.
Good track, actually.
Here's something we dug out of the archives.
It was recorded in December 1974,
and it's by Gentle Giant.
The band that started life as Simon Dupree
and The Big Sound in 1970 had a big...
Or rather, they started out...
When was it Simon Dupree and The Big Sound?
It was the middle 60s.
Then they had a hit in 67 called Kites.
And then in 1970, they became Gentle Giant.
And under the title of Gentle Giant, you heard them just there
doing a thing they recorded in December 1974 called Proclamation.
Better rather check a career when you think back on what they were doing.
They really did achieve far more success in the outside of our country
than they did within.
I remember vividly they did a lot around the university circuit
and entertained a lot of people.
It's 20 minutes to 11 on the Friday Rock Show.
July the 3rd, which is today, 10 years ago,
where Jim Morrison died in Paris.
Now, we're not playing any Doors tonight on the programme,
but I'd like to point you in the way of a programme
called Rock On on the air tomorrow between 5 and 6.30.
We've got a half-hour piece in Rock On tomorrow on the Doors
that features the three surviving members of the band
talking about Jim Morrison and how they affected him
and how, indeed, the whole band affected audiences.
So if you're a Jim Morrison and a Doors fan,
Rock On tomorrow, 5 to 6.30.
They'll be on the stage at Donington,
and I'll be right on the side of the stage listening to them
because I really like them.
Blackfoot, a track from their album Marauder.
It's just out on Atco.
That track was called Searchin'.
Before that, something which is brand new.
It's a single.
It's out on Rondelet Records.
It's by a group called Heritage.
It was entitled Strange Place to Be.
I'd like to say hello to loads of people
who made Nick Watkins' first year
at University College London a really good year.
He asked me to mention Magic Alice
and Steve, number one, Chang Sun.
Phil, the Fringe, Dave, the Deadhead,
Karen, Lucy, Judy, Andrew, P.C. Echo, I think that is, or Scho, possibly, S-C-H-O,
Gabrielle, Anne-Marie and everybody else that made his year a good one.
Giles Hooper is, along with a load of mates of his, I think at Scargill, it says here,
it says, mention Rise the Hippie and Kaz and Willie and Tom
and all the posers from the fourth and fifth year at Queen Elizabeth's Grammar School in Gainsborough.
Another string of names here, everybody who goes to Horsforth School, especially.
Ali and Beck and Rachel and Suzanne,
Alison, Alison, Sarah, Tim, Adam, Chris, Mandy, Paula,
Helen, Richard and Andy.
And finally, here's a real plea from the heart.
Postmarked, Portsmouth, Isle of Wight, 1st of July.
That's what it says on the postmark.
Portsmouth now with the Isle of Wight, I don't know.
Or maybe the Isle of Wight has drifted into the harbour.
Dear Tommy V, could you please say to Mike this?
Tell him that I love him very much
And I want him back, and I need him, please.
Thanks, Linda.
Mike, got the picture in the frame on the case?
The same name as if you didn't know,
for M.G. Whitehead of Deesborough and Northance,
and all his mates in Rothwell as well,
and also for Carolyn Bailey from Worthing.
I was going to say hello to Mike Bretherton,
and also for everybody in the third and fourth of years,

particularly Celeste DiMiranda,

Lydia Ward and Bob Chamberlain

it's a minute and a half after 11 o'clock

It says, please read out this request and tell Lonely Boy in Southampton
that Lonely Girl in Linz, Austria loves him very much.
And can't wait to get back from Austria next week and see him again.
Thanks, Lonely Boy, for five fantastic months.
Lots of love from Dougal, a.k.a. Die.
Right, now back to our archive session.
This is the other bit of the session that was recorded in December 1974 by Gentle Giant.
Listening back to that, I really believe they were completely underrated by their critics.
That's a really inventive piece of music.

actually on the 10th day of December 1974 in the BBC studios.
